data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0723 GMT - Astro Malaysia's operating environment remains challenging, Affin Hwang IB analysts write in a note. The satellite TV operator's introduction of "Astro One" bundles has slowed subscriber churn, among factors, signaling some early success in product bundling. However, the average revenue per user continues to fall at a faster pace, raising concerns over the sustainability of revenue and profitability even if subscriber losses moderate, the analysts say. Astro's radio and television advertising expenditure also remains soft. This suggests muted consumer and business sentiment, as U.S. tariffs and global trade uncertainties weigh on spending, the analysts say. Affin Hwang IB cuts Astro's target price to MYR0.08 from MYR0.13, while maintaining a sell rating on the stock. Shares areflat at MYR0.14.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0523 GMT - Inari Amertron's earnings outlook seems more positive to MBSB Research as its radio frequency segment utilization rate rises following new smartphone launches, which supports steady revenue. The recent joint acquisition of Lumiled could diversify the company's earnings and tap into global LED and MicroLED growth, although regulatory approval is pending, it says in a note. The company's efforts to expand advanced packaging solutions could accelerate with government fiscal support. However, significant results are likely to be seen in FY2027 rather than FY2026, it adds. MBSB raises Inari's target price to MYR2.12 from MYR1.98, while maintaining a neutral rating on the stock. Shares are 1.4% lower at MYR2.12. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0448 GMT - Life360's bulls at Morgan Stanley don't think that the location-app developer's price fully reflects the prospects for its new pet-tracking service. The MS analysts see potential upside in subscriber growth, retention and revenue from the bundling of the service to 11 million dog-owning families among Life360's user base. They also think that Life360 could launch new partnerships with third parties that increase the utility of its platform. They say in a note that they are looking forward to the dual-listed company's 3Q results in November and news on early take-up of the pet service. MS lifts its target price on Life360's Australia-listed stock 15% to A$58.50 and stays overweight. Shares are up 3.7% at A$52.87. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0441 GMT - UBTech Robotics has a bright outlook with rising capacity and sales momentum, Citi analysts say in a note. The company will boost its humanoid robot capacity to about 300 units a month by end-September, enough to support 2026 deliveries, the analysts say, citing management. UBTech expects to deliver more than 500 units in 2025 and 2,000-3,000 units in 2026, backed by strong intent orders across industries. Citi says the company has a first-mover advantage in industrial applications. Citi lifts its 2025 and 2026 revenue forecasts by 5% and 3%, upgrades the stock to buy from buy/high risk and raises its target price to HK$170.00 from HK$121.00. Shares last traded at HK$139.30. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0325 GMT - Tencent is likely to benefit from the recent surge in game approvals by Chinese authorities, Morningstar analyst Ivan Su write in a note. Su forecasts Tencent's revenue to grow 13.1% in 2025 and 10.4% in 2026 and adjusted EPS to rise 20.1% this year. The analyst notes that Chinese authorities approved 463 game licenses in 3Q, a 34% on-year increase and a 23% year-to-date rise through September, suggesting the Chinese gaming industry is on track for double-digit growth in the coming quarters. Morningstar views Tencent as undervalued, trading at a 20% discount to its valuation, maintaining its fair value estimates of $102.00 on Tencent's ADR, which last closed at $83.31. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0310 GMT - TSMC's 2025 revenue could beat its already lifted full-year guidance of 30% growth in U.S. dollar terms amid rising AI demand, Citi analysts say in a research note. TSMC could see even stronger demand for its advanced chips into 2027 thanks to the bigger integrated-circuit size for the next-generation chips and expected strong 2-nanometer chip ramp-up starting from 2H 2026, they say. TSMC's capex could reach US$47 billion and US$50 billion in 2026 and 2027, respectively, they say. Citi thinks TSMC will like reallocate more of its research and design resources and capacity to the most advanced nodes and advanced packaging. Maintaining a buy call on TSMC, Citi raises its target price to NT$1,600.00 from NT$1,400.00. Shares were recently at NT$1,300.00. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0236 GMT - Montage Technology is likely capitalizing on the memory migration cycle, Daiwa Capital Markets analysts say in a research report. It has strong competitiveness in the global memory interconnect chip market, with an improving product mix amid the migration to the DDR5 memory technology standard from DDR4. Its interconnect chip business is likely to grow over the next few years, with the technology migration requiring higher-value memory interconnect chips. It's also developing interconnect solutions related to Serializer-Deserializer high-speed serial interface technology for growth, they note. Daiwa initiates coverage of the stock with a buy rating and a target price of CNY165.00. Shares 0.6% higher at CNY137.54. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0104 GMT - Astro Malaysia's earnings outlook may remain weak given subdued consumer sentiment, Maybank IB analysts Nur Natasha Ariza and Yin Shao Yang say in a note. Although Astro expects advertisers' sentiment to improve as tariff uncertainties ease, the analysts are less optimistic. They highlight that the electricity tariff hike effective July and the lower RON95 subsidized fuel price are unlikely to provide a meaningful boost to ad demand. They cut Astro's FY2026-FY2028 core net profit forecasts by 39%-48%, as recent earnings came in below estimates. Maybank cuts Astro's target price to MYR0.07 from MYR0.12, while maintaining a sell rating on the stock. Shares are unchanged at MYR0.14. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2013 GMT - Reddit has a pipeline of new revenue opportunities at its disposal as it looks to close its monetization gap, Baird analysts say. The company's search business has a roughly $400 billion dollar domestic market opportunity, with a text-oriented app expected to contribute monetization next year, they say. Shopping ads are also a key medium-term opportunity as Reddit launches performance ads with measurable return on investment in the coming year, they say. The platform is also gaining traction internationally through machine translation, they add. Those opportunities, among others, continue to provide upside potential, they say, raising their price target on the stock to $240 from $177. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1821 GMT - Netflix is beefing up its ad business, most notably with a recent partnership with Amazon Ads that should support improved advertising onboarding, flexible buying, better performance and higher ad spend, say JPMorgan analysts. Netflix is also rolling out interactive ads and its Netflix Ads Suite should drive faster execution and improve measurement and targeting. The analysts say Netflix's Ad Tier is tracking ahead of management's expectations year-to-date and is on pace to roughly double advertising revenue in 2025. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1737 GMT - Netflix's original content and diversification can mitigate competitive impacts from media industry consolidation, say JPMorgan analysts. Paramount Skydance preparing a bid for Warner Bros. Discovery has sparked concern among Netflix investors that a larger Paramount would increase competition and hurt its ability to license third-party content. But 62% of Netflix's content assets are originals and no title receives more than 1% of total viewing, limiting the impact of consolidation. And while it's unlikely, Netflix could also participate in the consolidation in the long-term by acquiring a leading studio, giving it "access to a significant library of high quality content," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1535 GMT - The EU's Digital Markets Act is good for European consumers and should be protected, European consumer advocacy group BEUC says after Apple called for the bloc's executive arm to repeal the law. Apple said Wednesday that the rulebook--designed to curb the market power of the world's largest tech groups--is making it harder to bring new features to the region. "It is disappointing that Apple is continuing to resist change and trying to delay compliance every step of the way," Agustin Reyna, BEUC's director general, says. "Apple is misrepresenting what the DMA stands for: more competition and fairer digital markets which both consumers and businesses, especially SMEs, need," he says. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0809 GMT - U.S. President Trump's new round of sectoral tariff are likely to negatively affect highly trade-dependent economies like Singapore, DBS senior economist Chua Han Teng writes in an email. The measures, including a 100% tariff on branded or patented drug imports from Oct. 1, marks a continuation of Trump's protectionist agenda, he says. "Continued imposition of U.S. tariffs and ongoing uncertainty of new ones will hurt already weakened business sentiment," Chua says. He notes, however, the exemption for drugmakers building a production facility in the U.S. may mitigate the blow. Singapore hosts nine of the world's top 10 drugmakers, with major pharma firms already establishing or expanding manufacturing operations in the U.S., Chua notes.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0805 GMT - President Trump's drug tariffs will have a limited impact on big Swiss pharma players given their U.S. presence, Vontobel analysts write. While there is broad room for interpretation of Trump's announcement, Roche and Novartis are shielded by their U.S. footprints, while Sandoz should be unaffected since it focuses on exempted generic products, the analysts say. Galderma's exposure is less clear as it has a smaller U.S. presence. Tariffs will increase U.S. drug prices and supply concerns, but they shouldn't threaten the country's leading position in drug development, Vontobel says. Galderma shares fall 3.2%, with Roche and Novartis down 0.3% and 0.2%, respectively. Sandoz shares trade broadly flat. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0737 GMT - European pharmaceutical stocks fell in early trade after President Trump announced 100% tariffs on those companies that aren't building plants in the U.S. Swiss skincare company Galderma was the biggest faller, losing 3.55%, while Novo Nordisk shares are down 1.3%. AstraZeneca, Roche and Bayer shares were all down 0.2%. Tariffs are expected to start from Oct. 1.(ian.walker@wsj.com)

0639 GMT - The impact of U.S. medical-device tariffs on European med-tech companies hinges on whether products for disabled people will be exempted, Bernstein analysts write. Products for disabled people are exempted from tariffs under the Nairobi Protocol and the effect of tariffs on the sector will depend on whether levies can override it, according to Bernstein. If applicable, chronic-care specialists including Coloplast and ConvaTec would be more exposed to potential levies than hearing-aid makers including Sonova, Demant, GN Store Nord and Amplifon, the analysts say. Another critical factor is manufacturing location. A recent U.S.-EU trade agreement caps tariffs on medicines at 15%, but it is unclear if similar agreements would shield medical technologies, Bernstein writes. Convatec fell 5.6%, Demant 4.8%, and GN 6.6% on Thursday. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0441 GMT - The U.S.'s fresh tariffs on pharmaceutical imports aren't a big concern for China's healthcare sector, according to Jefferies analysts in a research note. Chinese pharmaceutical companies have minimal sales exposure to the U.S. market, the analysts say. Investors may have concerns regarding Chinese biotechs' U.S. expansion, but at this stage, most companies are at least 1-2 years away from potential U.S. commercialization, they note. Also, most Chinese biotechs have U.S. partners and are immune to the tariffs, so they barely dent China's healthcare powerhouses, the analysts add. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0232 GMT - IHH Healthcare is expected to post steady 2H earnings before stronger growth in 2026 on strength in its key regions, Affin Hwang IB analyst Andrew Lim says in a note. Singapore continues to be IHH's biggest earnings driver, with growth in 2026 supported by Mount Elizabeth Hospital reopening its beds. Payor pressures in Malaysia may cap margins, but India is set to deliver the fastest growth, supported by Fortis's bright prospects and aggressive five-year bed expansion plan, he says. Lim is positive on IHH's outlook amid rising inpatient demand, stable margins and a demand-driven expansion, supported by growing healthcare awareness. Affin Hwang maintains a buy rating on IHH and keeps its target price at MYR8.10. Shares are 0.8% higher at MYR7.43. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0141 GMT - Malaysia's healthcare sector is poised for sustained long-term growth, driven by rising noncommunicable diseases and an aging population, CIMB Securities analyst Walter Aw Lik Hsin says in a note. Private hospital bed capacity is expected to grow 4%-5% annually through 2028, narrowing the gap with developed markets, he says. A government campaign to attract foreign travelers is set to boost healthcare tourism, reinforcing long-term sector growth, he adds. The sector's near-term challenges include manpower shortages and regulatory uncertainty, but private operators like IHH Healthcare and KPJ Healthcare are better positioned to attract talent. CIMB maintains an overweight rating on the sector, with IHH Healthcare as its top pick due to its diversified assets. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2334 GMT [Dow Jones]--Volatility in Telix Pharmaceuticals's share price is set to continue — this time for positive reasons. That's the view of Bell Potter after Telix secured transitional pass through, or TPT, status for its Gozellix imaging agent for prostate cancer in the U.S. "The decision to pursue this strategy (for a refresh on the pass through) has proven to be a master stroke by the company and one that has shown its major competitor in the PSMA imaging market asleep at the wheel," analyst John Hester says. TPT status on Gozellix is effective Oct. 1. Bell Potter assumes an average reimbursement of $5,000 per patient. It retains a buy call on Telix and A$23.00/share price target. Telix ended Thursday at A$15.38. (david.winning@wsj.com)

2302 GMT - Macquarie stays bullish on Monash IVF as it looks beyond the in vitro fertilization specialist's recent troubles, which include an embryo mix-up at a clinic and an earnings downgrade. Macquarie says these are captured in Monash IVF's share price, which is down some 46% over the past 12 months. "We see upside over the medium-term from an improving macro environment, increased genetic testing, underlying structural demands, demographic and social changes," Macquarie says. It retains a A$1.00/share price target. Monash IVF ended Thursday at A$0.655. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0741 GMT - Moshi Moshi Retail is expected to maintain its market leadership despite rising competition, supported by differentiated products, efficient sourcing and a large store network, Maybank Securities says. Analyst Suttatip Peerasub projects gross margin improvement in 2025-2026 from higher-margin products and better supplier terms as production scales. Maybank forecasts 3Q net profit growth of 13% and sales growth of 15% on solid same-store sales and store additions, with full-year earnings projected to rise 19% in 2025 and 16% in 2026. Moshi also deserves a premium valuation given its three-year EPS CAGR of 16.7%, more than double the sector average of 7.6%, Peerasub adds. Maybank maintains a buy rating and THB51.00 target on Moshi's stock, which was recently at THB40.75.(jason.chau@wsj.com)

0440 GMT - Morgan Stanley analysts think that some observers are missing the size of the likely second-half skew in Macquarie's earnings. Their forecast for 6% first-half earnings growth significantly lags behind consensus, but they think that analysts elsewhere aren't seeing the full picture. They say in a note that a recent client compensation agreement and risks of green-energy asset write-downs could also weigh on profit. However, they think that a recovery in global capital markets and commodities revenues should drive a powerful second-half recovery. They are looking for 12% earnings growth across the full year, and their annual net profit forecast is broadly in line with consensus. MS stays equal-weight on the stock and lifts its target price by 4.6% to A$226.00. Shares are up 0.4% at A$216.82. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2312 GMT [Dow Jones]--Fonterra Shareholders' Fund sets a high bar for future earnings, prompting Macquarie to raise its price target on the dairy cooperative by 17% to NZ$8.61/share. Fonterra has agreed to the sale of its consumer businesses, known as Mainland Group, to Lactalis for NZ$3.85 billion. It expects to take three years to replace the hit to operating profit from that deal. Fonterra is targeting 5% annual Ebit growth to FY 2028, the bank says. Its new price target reflects the Mainland sale and higher sustainable earnings implied in the Ebit target, although it is less bullish about this goal than management. Fonterra is up 1.3% at NZ$7.91 today. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1551 GMT - Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem says economic policy from the country's governments have leaned too heavily on fiscal-stimulus measures designed to increase consumer demand. "We've overrelied on priming demand and not spent enough time on the tougher decisions of structural reform and making the investments that we need to actually grow the economy over the long term," says Macklem, according to remarks in an interview with Bloomberg News. His comments somewhat echo a speech he delivered this week, in which he warned Canada could either remain static and suffer income losses from US trade policy, or implement structural reforms and champion the building on new trade corridors to help set the stage for future growth. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1401 GMT - Pending U.S. home sales dipped roughly 1% from a year earlier during the four weeks ending Sept. 21, the first decline in nearly three months, according to Redfin. The weekly average mortgage rate has fallen to 6.26%, the lowest level in nearly a year and down from roughly 6.9% at the start of summer. The dip was in anticipation of the Fed's first interest-rate cut of the year. But steadily falling mortgage rates haven't brought many homebuyers to the market. And while mortgage applications to refinance homes jumped 58% week over week during the second week of September, mortgage-purchase applications rose just 3%. Homebuying demand isn't yet improving because of stubbornly high home prices, still-high mortgage rates, and lack of new homes to choose from.(chris.wack@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0732 GMT - China's new 2035 target of 3,600GW of solar and wind power capacity looks conservative and will likely be exceeded, Deutsche Bank analyst Gary Zhou says. He expects supply-side reform to remain the near-term focus, as restoring supply-demand balance in the renewable sector is key for investors. On the demand side, Zhou forecasts China will add 1,455GW of solar and 600GW of wind power capacities during the 2026-2030 period, well above additions in 2021-2025. DB's top renewables pick is GCL Tech, citing its low valuation and potential gains from solar supply reforms. Shares last traded at HK$1.27. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

2317 GMT [Dow Jones]--Carnarvon Energy now owns 18-19% of Strike Energy's equity following the latter's capital raising, effectively securing a blocking stake in the Western Australia-focused natural- gas producer. That means Carnarvon "is now integral in any commercial negotiations if Hancock was seeking to fully consolidate the Perth basin Kingia acreage," says Macquarie. Hancock bought 50% of the West Erregulla natural-gas field in 2023 for A$450 million. Strike Energy owns the other 50% of West Erregulla. Macquarie resumes coverage of Strike Energy with a neutral call and A$0.10/share price target. Strike Energy ended Thursday at A$0.105. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1919 GMT - Crude futures recover early losses and end the session mixed with continued concerns over Russian supplies keeping sellers cautious. "There's no doubt that right now that the Russian hits on their oil infrastructure by Ukraine means that that really is Ground Zero," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. With Europe planning tougher sanctions on Russian oil and gas, it "could create a situation where we could start to see significant change in market psychology and a potential breakout to the upside." WTI slips 1 cent to $64.98 a barrel, and Brent settles up 0.2% at $69.42. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1750 GMT - Oil futures are pulling up from earlier lows with continued support from tensions over the Russia-Ukraine war. Yesterday's WTI break above the 200-day moving average was "a technical signal that suggests a bullish market trend," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. WTI briefly retested the 200-day moving average as support, "and successfully held the level." he adds. WTI is off 0.1% at $64.91 a barrel and Brent is near flat at $69.29. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1343 GMT - Activity in Canada's natural resources sector weakened sharply in the last quarter. Real GDP in the sector contracted 2.4% in 2Q, a reversal after growth of 1.8% in the first three months, Statistics Canada data shows. That was the largest decrease since 2Q 2020 and significantly deeper than the 0.4% decline in economy-wide GDP for the quarter. The declines for the sector were broad, with a notable 4.9% fall for forestry, 2.5% drop for energy and 1.2% decline for minerals and mining. The quarter was marked by an overall 4% fall in natural resources prices, including a steep decline for crude oil, and a 6.6% slump in sector export volumes even as imports increased 6.6%. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1310 GMT - Oil futures pull back after two sessions of gains with increasing tensions over the Russia-Ukraine war keeping prices up despite concerns about imminent oversupply. Russia risk "makes shorting many parts of the complex very tricky indeed," Neil Crosby of Sparta Commodities says in a note. While the market looks better supplied post-summer and macroeconomic risk is rising, Russia and wider geopolitical risk is also higher, he says. "No wonder flat price has been so range-bound this summer." Of the opposing upside and downside risks, "at the moment it does look like the Ukraine situation is winning out, but the mood may change by next week." WTI is down 0.5% at $64.68 a barrel, and Brent is off 0.4% at $69.04.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0632 GMT - Lotte Chemical's earnings could start to recover in 2028 after completing its business restructuring, KB Securities analysts Wooje Chun and Yunju Song write in a note. The South Korean company is still reeling from the worst-ever global chemical-industry downturn, and is expected to complete its ongoing business consolidation by 2027, they say. The analysts note that global naphtha cracking center operators in Europe, South Korea, China and Japan shut down some of their facilities for four years until 2027 to address the oversupply. KB expects Lotte Chemical's operating loss to narrow to KRW287.5 billion in 2026. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0453 GMT - The price of copper "will need to rise dramatically" to spur enough investment in new mines to meet demand, Shaw and Partners analysts say in a note. Bringing on new copper supply is challenging and production costs are likely to rise, say the analysts. "Expansions of existing mines are becoming increasingly expensive, and new projects often involve lower-grade ore and higher development costs," they say. "This is compounded by the slow pace of new discoveries and stricter environmental and social standards, creating a bottleneck that prevents a rapid increase in supply to meet growing demand." Shaw expects copper to average $10,524/metric ton next year, versus consensus of $9,786/ton and spot of $10,287/ton. The broker's key copper stock picks are AIC Mines, Aurelia Metals and Sunstone Metals. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0212 GMT - Liontown Resources provides some additional disclosures on its FY 2025 results call, including that it expects capital expenditure of A$55 million-A$70 million from FY 2028 onward, Citi analyst Kate McCutcheon says in a note. That compares to Citi's expectation of roughly A$80 million a year, says McCutcheon. Liontown also flags flat depreciation and amortization in FY 2026 year over year, before a moderation in FY 2027, she adds. Liontown's FY 2025 revenue is in line with expectations, and Citi makes only minor changes to its estimates following the results, McCutcheon says. Citi has a sell rating on Liontown. It raises its target to A$0.50 from A$0.45. Liontown is up 6.1% at A$0.96. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0128 GMT - Geo Energy Resources stands to benefit from the construction of a 92-kilometer hauling road and a jetty in Indonesia's South Sumatra, Phillip Securities Research's Paul Chew says in commentary. There is no change to the projected completion and commissioning by mid-2026, with an additional two months needed to become fully operational. Once completed, coal production at the Singapore-listed Indonesian company's TRA mine in South Sumatra is expected to increase to 12 million metric tons in 2027 from 2 million metric tons in 2025. The brokerage raises its target price on the stock to S$0.59 from S$0.47 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 1.1% higher at S$0.455. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0120 GMT - Growth in the rare-earth supply chain outside of China depends on manufacturers and governments being receptive to higher prices for rare earths produced elsewhere, Morgan Stanley analyst Rahul Anand says in a note. Anand's remarks follow a Reuters report that says G-7 nations and the European Union are considering price floors to encourage production. Already, the U.S. has set a price floor for some light rare earths in a deal with MP Materials. Australian officials say they are also considering price floors for critical minerals. MS remains overweight on Australia's Lynas and Iluka "as key beneficiaries of higher ex-China rare earths prices," says Anand. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1827 GMT - Gold futures settle slightly higher on strong physical metal flow. Gold ETFs have seen inflows totaling $10.5 billion in September, says Citi Research in a note. Year-to-date, the flow to ETFs is roughly $50 billion. "ETF has outshined all other gold demand sectors this year and is the single most important contributor to the gold price rally in our view," says Kenny Hu of Citi Research. Front-month gold futures close up 0.1% to $3,736.90 a troy ounce, for the third-highest finish on record. Today marks the fourth positive session in the past five for gold. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1746 GMT - Worthington Steel's hot-rolled coil pricing is pressured by softer volumes in most of its end markets, resulting in wider holding losses, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams tells analysts. Current pricing is about $800 per ton, down from a peak of $950 in March, he says. The steel processor expects to generate inventory holding losses in its F2Q of $5 million to $10 million, compared with the $5.6 million in the current quarter, Adams says. The lower prices come as tariffs on imported steel increased in June. Worthington Steel slides 7% to $31.54. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1716 GMT - Worthington Steel's automotive market is finally making a comeback, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams says during an analyst call. The sector was one of the few bright spots during F1Q, as shipments increased 17% from the prior year. The auto market itself is improving and the company now expects to finish the year with a 15 million unit build rate, up from prior forecasts of 13.5 million. Worthington also anticipates tailwinds from potential interest rate cuts. Beyond improving industry trends, Worthington has also won market share by ramping up volumes and new programs, executives say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1701 GMT - Freeport-McMoRan's stock offers an attractive buying opportunity as share falls on lowered production forecasts after a mudslide at one of its mines, Jefferies analysts say in a note. While the mudslide is a clear negative for the company, some of this downside was already reflected in the share price because the market had been expecting a downgrade to guidance, the analysts say. The disruption should also lead to a tighter copper market and a higher copper price, which would be positive for the company's operations in the Americas. "We would buy FCX on today's weakness," they say. The stock falls 5%, to $35.78, extending its week-to-date decline to 21%.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1652 GMT - Worthington Steel is facing soft demand due to macroeconomic uncertainty, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams tells analysts. Agriculture is the most challenged market, as volumes were down 50% year-over-year due to continued softness in the agricultural equipment sector, Adams says. Shipments to the heavy truck market fell 7% and to construction customers fell 3%. Toll processing volumes fell 22% because of less processing from mills and service centers, as well as the closure of a processing plant in Ohio, Adams says. "We experienced pluses and minuses across various markets as customers continued to navigate uncertainty during the quarter," he said. Shares fall 6.4%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1639 GMT - Freeport-McMoRan 2027 production levels could also be at risk after the company cut its guidance for this year and next year after a fatal mudslide at one of its mines, Jefferies analysts say in a note. Management now expects negligible sales volumes at the mine in the fourth quarter and is guiding to as much as 35% lower volumes in 2026 compared to its prior forecast, the analysts say. "There is downside risk to 2027 production as well, in our view," they say, noting the Grasberg mine is one of the largest and lowest cost mines in the world, accounting for about 3% of global mined copper supply. They lower their price target on the stock to $46 from $48. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1634 GMT - Freeport-McMoRan's forecast for a hit to production from a recent mudslide at its flagship Indonesia copper-gold mine is worse than expected, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. The company's initial view for next year points to production at the mine that could be about 35% below prior guidance, marking the second major setback for the mine after a smelter fire last year, the analysts say. The analysts establish a December 2026 price target on the stock of $46, from their prior December 2025 target of $56.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0748 GMT - Bernstein initiates coverage on Rheinmetall's stock with a market-perform rating and a target price of 1,960 euros. The bank believes that the German arms maker will continue to win orders in the coming years, as demand outstrips supply and early capacity increases drive the growth. "But after the first phase of replenishment and during the transition to long-cycle investments, we worry a perfect storm in short-cycle (armoured vehicles, shells) products." Bernstein says that a transition to a long-cycle portfolio will be long and costly. Analysts expect more attractive risk-rewards for players that are already positioned, at cheaper valuations. Bernstein's top pick is BAE Systems. Rheinmetall shares are down 0.3% at 1,972.50 euros. (alistair.macdonald@wsj.com)

0739 GMT - Safran will get a boost from Turkish Airlines' Boeing aircraft purchase, as the French manufacturer produces the Leap engine for the 737 MAX and equipment for the 787 plane, Jefferies analysts say. Turkish Airlines said Friday that it decided to purchase a total of 75 aircraft from Boeing, consisting of 50 firm and 25 option orders for 787-9 and 787-10 models with deliveries scheduled between 2029 and 2034. Negotiations with Boeing regarding the purchase of a total of 150 aircraft, consisting of 100 firm and 50 option orders for the 737-8/10 MAX models, have been completed, the carrier said. Safran shares are up 1.2% at 295.60 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0718 GMT - President Trump's announcement of 25% tariffs on imported heavy trucks is light on detail, but Daimler Truck could suffer the most given current production in the U.S. and overall exposure to the U.S. at the group level, Bernstein analysts write. U.S. manufacturer Paccar could be the greatest beneficiary, it adds. Bernstein notes the tariffs apply from Oct. 1, so they won't impact the third quarter, where orders and volumes are weak for all manufacturers. It adds that further inflationary increases to truck prices will negatively impact all, especially with new truck prices currently considerably elevated after many years of price increases. It rates Volvo and Daimler Truck at underperform and Traton at market-perform. Volvo shares rise 2.1%, Daimler Truck shares fall 4.4% and Traton shares drop 2.1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0652 GMT - The Philippine port sector's outlook appears positive, Maybank Securities' Germaine Guinto says in a research report. The ports' container volumes gained strong momentum in 1H and are poised to quicken in 2H, the analyst says. Growth is anchored by resilient domestic consumption and a pickup in trade activity, with foreign containers accounting for the majority of the ports' traffic, the analyst says. International Container Terminal Services, via Manila International Container Terminal port's dominant gateway role, remains best-placed to capture this momentum, the analyst adds. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of PHP590.00 on International Container Terminal Services shares, which are 0.6% lower at PHP495.80. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1905 GMT - Electric vehicle sales in the U.S. are expected to hit a record of around 409,000 in the third quarter, as consumers take advantage of government-backed incentives slated to end Sept. 30, Cox Automotive said. The prior record was set in the fourth quarter of 2024, with 365,824 units sold, according to Cox. The share of EV sales in the third quarter will likely also be a record, coming in at close to 10% of total sales, Cox added. "The federal tax credit was a key catalyst for EV adoption, and its expiration marks a pivotal moment," said Stephanie Valdez Streaty, director of Industry Insights at Cox Automotive. "This shift will test whether the electric vehicle market is mature enough to thrive on its own fundamentals or still needs support to expand further." Sales of EVs are expected to slow in the fourth quarter, although long-term sales growth will continue, Cox said. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1856 GMT - Cox Automotive said the September new-vehicle sale pace is expected to be around 16.2 million, up from 15.8 million in September 2024 and higher than August's 16.1 million. Year over year, third-quarter sales volume is expected to rise 6.2%, reaching 4.14 million. "The new-vehicle sales pace has been surprisingly strong this summer and through the third quarter as uncertainty around tariff policy has decreased," said Charlie Chesbrough, senior economist at Cox Automotive. "Continued low inflation and unemployment rates, coupled with a strong stock market, have kept consumers in a buying mood," Chesbrough added. Sales numbers have been helped by an increase in EV sales, as buyers move to make purchases before tax credits expire at the end of September, Chesbrough said. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1716 GMT - Worthington Steel's automotive market is finally making a comeback, Chief Financial Officer Tim Adams says during an analyst call. The sector was one of the few bright spots during F1Q, as shipments increased 17% from the prior year. The auto market itself is improving and the company now expects to finish the year with a 15 million unit build rate, up from prior forecasts of 13.5 million. Worthington also anticipates tailwinds from potential interest rate cuts. Beyond improving industry trends, Worthington has also won market share by ramping up volumes and new programs, executives say. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1547 GMT - CarMax says its loan loss provisions for 2Q rose to $142.2 million from $112.6 million the previous year, citing worsening performance among 2022 and 2023 vintage loans. CEO Bill Nash says on the analyst call that in 2022 and 2023, consumers were flush with cash from the pandemic and hadn't yet fully experienced inflation. They then started to struggle more, leading CarMax to adjust its extension policy. Nash says that with those extensions "we wanted to see how much was going to come back in delinquency. And unfortunately, during the quarter, we saw more revert back to delinquency and loss."(nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1539 GMT - CarMax is seeing a pullback in used car buying from high-credit score customers and better sales among customers with lower credit scores. "Your mid to high FICO customers, they seem to be sitting on the sidelines a little bit," CEO Bill Nash says on the analyst call. "The one area that's maybe not down is probably low FICO 550 and below." The company says that division by credit score was becoming clear throughout the credit industry. "We can talk to other lenders, other dealers. You can see it in the credit bureaus. It's apparent," says Jon Daniels, executive vice president of CarMax Auto Finance. CarMax slides 20% after sales and profits plunged in the latest quarter. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.description:
26.9.2025 10:20:26 CEST | Vend Marketplaces ASA | Mandatory notification oftrade primary insiders

Today, the primary insider in Vend Marketplaces ASA, Maria Sandgren, has beengranted a gross amount of 143 B-shares, corresponding to a net amount of 64B-shares*, in the form of restricted stock units in connection with thelong-term incentive plan (the "Vend LTI Plan").

The B-shares will be transferred in three tranches - one third after one year,one third after two years, and one third after three years. For moreinformationon the Vend LTI Plan, please refer to Vend Marketplaces ASA's RemunerationReport 2024.

Please see the attached form(s) for notification and public disclosure oftransactions, including net number* of restricted stock units granted toprimaryinsiders.

* Net number of shares to be received on the vesting date, at the end of the3-year programme period. The net number of shares is partially based on eachparticipant's current marginal tax rate. The actual number of shares receivedatthe vesting date may vary, should the participant's margin tax rate at thevesting date have changed.

data.title: Indonesian rupiah rangebound after cenbank steps in; Asian stocks tumble
data.description:
Key points:
  • South Korea shares fall 3%
  • Indonesia rupiah rangebound, central bank intervenes
  • Thai baht set for biggest weekly drop since mid-July
  • Trump announces new tariffs from October 1

By Himanshi Akhand

Indonesia's rupiah was rangebound on Friday after the central bank stepped in to prevent it from sliding further from its five-month lows, while most Asian currencies were under pressure as traders pared back bets of sharp U.S. rate cuts.

Regional stocks tumbled after U.S. President Donald Trump announced new import tariffs, including 100% duties on branded drugs.

A sell-off in Asian drugmakers was led by South Korea's Samsung Biologics and SK Biopharmaceuticals , which lost 2.1% and 3.5%, respectively. As a result, Seoul's KOSPI index tumbled nearly 3% in its steepest one-day decline in eight weeks.

MSCI's gauge of Asian emerging market equities (.MIMS00000PUS) slipped as much as 1.4%, set for its worst day since August 1 and was on track for its first weekly loss in four weeks.

Taiwan stocks TWSE:TAIEX fell as much as 2% in their third consecutive session of losses, while Indian stocks dropped 0.5%, driven by losses in pharmaceutical shares.

Singapore is the most economically exposed to U.S. pharmaceutical tariffs in EM Asia; meanwhile India might be cushioned by its overall domestic orientation despite the importance of pharmaceuticals in its U.S. exports, Barclays analysts said.

In Indonesia, the rupiah was last down 0.2%. It fell as much as 0.3% to 16,790 per dollar early in the session but inched up around midday after the central bank governor committed to maintaining the currency's stability, including continued intervention.

Bank Indonesia's surprise interest rate cut last week, viewed as bowing to President Prabowo Subianto's push to accelerate growth, exacerbated pressure on a currency already reeling under risks of fiscal slippage.

Barclays' analysts expect the rupiah to trade in the 16,600 to 16,900 per dollar range over a one-year horizon.

"We expect Bank Indonesia to allow the IDR to act as a shock absorber, but lean against the pace of depreciation if it is too volatile or too fast."

South Korean won weakened to its lowest since mid-May, extending losses beyond the key 1,400-per-dollar level it breached on Thursday. The selling gained momentum after President Lee Jae Myung said the country would need a currency swap agreement with the U.S. if it accepted Washington's demands to invest $350 billion in America as part of the trade deal.

The MSCI index of emerging market currencies (.MIEM00000CUS) fell 0.2% in its seventh straight session of losses.

The Indian rupee held above its all-time low, supported by likely central bank intervention.

The Philippine peso was down 2.1% for the week, its worst weekly performance in three years. The Thai baht was set for a 1.2% weekly drop, its worst since late July.

HIGHLIGHTS:

** Thailand's credit rating not a concern, no VAT hike plan, official says

** South Korea begins new trial of ousted President Yoon over failed martial law

** US consumer spending data release due later on Friday

data.title: Gaza aid flotilla set to head east from Greece despite Israeli warnings
data.description:
Key points:
  • Israel has said flotilla will not pass
  • Boats attacked by drones earlier this week
  • Italy and Spain sent naval ships to the flotilla

An international aid flotilla was set to leave Greek waters and head towards Gaza on Friday, organisers said, defying warnings from Israel that it would use any means to block the boats' access to the war-torn enclave.

The Global Sumud Flotilla is using about 50 civilian boats to try to break Israel's naval blockade of Gaza, an initiative Israel strongly opposes. Many lawyers, parliamentarians, and activists, including Swedish climate campaigner Greta Thunberg, are on board.

Its passage across the Mediterranean has raised international tensions, especially after it said it was attacked by drones this week. No one was hurt, but Italy and Spain have dispatched naval ships to provide assistance to their and other European citizens on the flotilla.

Greece said it would guarantee the safe sailing of the flotilla off Greece, but Friday's launch will take the flotilla back into international waters in the eastern Mediterranean. Organisers said the boats aim to arrive early next week.

'TRYING TO DELIVER HOPE AND SOLIDARITY'

Israel, which has imposed a naval blockade on Gaza, said the flotilla will not pass and that the project will only assist Hamas.

The flotilla has blamed Israel for the drone attack. Israel's foreign ministry did not respond directly to the accusation, but invited the flotilla to drop humanitarian aid for Israel to take to Gaza, or face consequences.

The exact timing of the departure from Greece is unclear. Organisers said on Friday that one of its lead boats had suffered a mechanical failure but that it was still preparing to depart.

"We are not just delivering humanitarian aid. We are trying to deliver hope and solidarity, to send a strong message that the world stands with Palestine," Thunberg said from the deck of a boat off the Greek island of Crete on Thursday.

Italy had proposed a compromise whereby aid supplies could be dropped off in Cyprus and handed over to the Catholic Church's Latin Patriarchate of Jerusalem, which would then distribute it in Gaza.

Israel said it backed the idea but the flotilla rejected it.

ITALY WARNS FLOTILLA AGAINST CONTINUING

Italy's foreign ministry has sent a message to Italian members of the flotilla warning them against continuing with the mission, and offering help with repatriation if they choose to disembark in Greece.

It said the navy ship it has deployed would intervene only for sea rescue or humanitarian operations, and would "under no circumstances" engage in defensive or offensive military maneuvers against anyone.

"Whoever (continues with the mission) takes on all risks and is personally responsible for them," the ministry told activists.

Israel launched its nearly two-year-old war in Gaza in response to the October 7, 2023, attacks on the country by Hamas militants which killed some 1,200 people and saw 251 taken hostage, according to Israeli tallies.

Since then, the Israeli offensive has killed more than 65,000 Palestinians, according to Gaza health authorities, destroyed most buildings, and displaced the population, in many cases multiple times, while famine has taken hold in parts of the territory.

data.title: The Fed's Preferred Inflation Gauge, PCE, Is Due Today. Here's What to Know. — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation measure, the personal-consumption expenditures price index, is due out at 8:30 a.m. ET. Here's what to know:

  • In the 12 months through August, PCE likely rose 2.7%, forecasters polled by The Wall Street Journal estimate. That would be up from 2.6% in July.
  • On a core basis, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, the rise is expected to be 2.9%, the same rate as in July.
  • The Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic Analysis publishes the data. It is based on official price measures already published for August, so forecasters' guesses are usually very close to target.
  • Another widely followed inflation gauge, the consumer-price index, accelerated last month.

The Fed aims to keep inflation at a 2% annual rate over the longer run. Inflation is a key factor for its target interest rate, which it cut last week for the first time in nine months. On Tuesday, Fed Chair Jerome Powell suggested there was scope for further cuts this year if labor-market softness outweighs inflationary pressures.

The Fed's next rate decision is due Oct. 29.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Malaysia asks U.S. for zero tariff rate on furniture, automotive and aerospace parts
data.description:
Malaysia has asked the United States to consider imposing zero tariff rates on its exports of furniture, automotive and aerospace products, a Malaysian trade ministry official said on Friday.

Washington has also agreed to consider granting tariff exemptions to Malaysia for commodities not produced in the United States, including cocoa and palm oil, with a decision expected to be finalised next month, the official told Reuters, clarifying an earlier report by state news agency Bernama.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday announced a new round of tariffs on imported goods, including a 50% tariff on imported kitchen cabinets and bathroom vanities and a 30% tariff on upholstered furniture.

Malaysia and the United States are seeking to complete an agreement on tariffs after Washington imposed a 19% levy on Malaysian imports in August.

Malaysia Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said in a Facebook post on Thursday that the countries were targeting a deal before a planned October visit by Trump to Kuala Lumpur for a summit of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations.

data.title: Italian Consumer Morale Above Forecasts
data.description:
The Italian consumer confidence index rose to 96.8 in September 2025 from 96.2 in the previous month, surpassing market expectations of 96.5.

Sentiment improved across all components, with the personal climate index increasing slightly to 96 from 95.9, the current climate index climbing to 99.9 from 99.2, and the future climate index rising to 92.6 from 92.2.

Additionally, consumers’ assessment of the general economic situation strengthened, jumping to 98.8 in September from 97 in the previous month.

data.title: Italy Manufacturing Confidence Steady in September
data.description:
The manufacturing confidence index in Italy held steady at 87.3 in September 2025, slightly below market forecasts of 87.5.

Assessments of order books improved, while inventories of finished goods were seen as broadly unchanged from the previous month.

Meanwhile, expectations for future production deteriorated.

data.title: SNG: US plans to extend Africa trade deal by a year, says Lesotho minister
data.description:
Marafaele Mohloboli

Lesotho's trade minister said on Wednesday, 24 September 2025 that the US plans to extend the African Growth and Opportunity Act (Agoa), which gives the continent preferential access to US markets, by a year, after returning from a visit to Washington.

A slew of tariffs that US President Donald Trump imposed on global trading partners on 4 April hit African countries hard. They were widely seen as the death knell for the quarter-century-old Agoa deal, putting millions of livelihoods at risk.

Lesotho initially got hit with the world's highest tariff of 50% on Trump's so-called "Liberation Day" - ruinous for the tiny mountain kingdom's export-led development model, which was almost entirely dependent on textile factories selling jeans and T-shirts to the US

Trump reduced it to 15% in August. A Lesotho trade delegation visited the US from 15 September 2025 to 19 September 2025.

Minister of Trade, Industry and Business Development Mokhethi Shelile, who led the delegation, told a news conference late on Wednesday that they met US officials responsible for Agoa on the House of Representatives Ways and Means Committee and the Senate Finance Committee.

"They all agreed that Agoa has to be extended and they promised us that by November or December [at] the latest, it will be extended by a year," Shelile said.

Agoa expires on 30 September 2025 and companies that benefit from it have warned that any delay in renewing it risked significant job losses and factory closures.

Lesotho qualifies for textile and apparel benefits under Agoa, according to the Office of the United States Trade Representative. US goods and services trade with Lesotho totaled $276m in 2024, up 4.6% from a year earlier.

A White House spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

"We will be ... monitoring closely ... that the extension comes into force as promised, because if it doesn't, we are risking losing more jobs," Shelile said.

A spokesman for Democrats on the Senate Finance Committee, who are led by Oregon Senator Ron Wyden, noted: "The Trump administration hasn't informed Finance Committee Democrats [of] its position on renewing Agoa. Ranking Member Wyden continues to support renewing the program."
